critic_raw:
This is a carefully designed experiment that handles many common pitfalls. The temporal split, article versioning, popularity stratification, and baseline parity are all solid choices. I'll focus on the genuine methodological issues rather than manufacturing concerns.

**Issue 1: Stopword removal and stemming are antithetical to fine-tuning a pretrained transformer**

The methodology claims both models use 'identical tokenization and preprocessing,' which includes removing stopwords and applying stemming before feeding text to BERT/DistilBERT. This is a fundamental misconfiguration. Pretrained transformer models are trained on natural language with stopwords and full word forms intact; their attention mechanisms and subword tokenization (WordPiece, BPE) are calibrated to that input distribution. Removing stopwords and applying stemming before the transformer tokenizer will corrupt the input in unpredictable ways—stemmed tokens may not appear in the vocabulary, mid-word truncation can produce nonsense subwords, and the model's implicit syntactic reasoning (which relies on function words) will be degraded. This is not a minor performance hit; it changes what the model is actually doing.

Critically, this creates an asymmetric disadvantage: stemming and stopword removal are well-motivated for BM25 (they reduce vocabulary sparsity and term mismatch), but they are actively harmful for transformers. If this preprocessing is applied identically to both, the transformer is operating under conditions that degrade its core capability while BM25 operates under conditions that enhance its core capability. Any performance comparison under this setup does not measure 'BM25 vs. semantic transformer'—it measures 'BM25 under favorable preprocessing vs. transformer under degraded preprocessing.'

Evidence one way or the other: Run an ablation where the transformer receives raw (non-stemmed, non-stopword-removed) text versus preprocessed text. If MRR drops substantially on preprocessed input, this confirms the flaw. The BM25 baseline should use its own preprocessing pipeline; the transformer should use its own. Claiming 'identical preprocessing' as a methodological virtue is backwards here.

**Issue 2: Label definition excludes a systematic subset of hard cases**

Tickets with multiple articles, escalations, or unresolved status are excluded. This exclusion is operationally convenient but introduces a systematic bias: the excluded tickets are precisely the ones where retrieval is hardest—ambiguous, complex, or novel issues. The model is trained and evaluated only on tickets with clean, single-article resolutions, which likely skew toward common, well-defined issues.

The failure mode is that the experiment will overestimate real-world utility. In deployment, the model will encounter the full ticket distribution including escalations and multi-article cases. The experiment cannot speak to those cases at all. More subtly, the training signal is derived from the easiest subset of tickets, which may cause the model to learn shortcuts (article popularity, simple keyword overlap) that work on the easy set but fail on the hard set.

This is acknowledged partly under 'agent labeling noise' but is actually a selection bias problem, not a noise problem. Evidence: Report what fraction of all tickets are excluded and whether excluded tickets cluster in particular categories. If exclusion rate is >30% and concentrated in specific categories, the performance estimates are not representative of production load.

**Issue 3: Near-duplicate deduplication is applied asymmetrically**

The design removes near-duplicate tickets from validation/test that have high similarity to training tickets, but it does not describe removing duplicates within the validation and test sets themselves, nor does it clarify whether duplicates within training are removed. The stated goal is to 'prevent inflated performance from memorizing common issues,' but if the same ticket (or near-identical tickets) appears multiple times in the test set, MRR is inflated regardless of whether training examples are involved—the model sees a de facto upweighting of whatever article resolves that common issue.

This is a secondary concern but the mechanism is real: a highly common ticket type that appears 50 times in the test set contributes 50 reciprocal rank values to the MRR average, so performance on that single issue type dominates the aggregate metric. The per-category stratification partially mitigates this, but only if the deduplication is applied within test as well.

Evidence: Report the deduplication rate and whether it is applied within splits as well as across splits.

**Issue 4: Jargon standardization mapping fitted on training set has ambiguous scope**

The jargon mapping (e.g., 'HIPAA compliance' → 'HIPAA_compliance') is described as 'curated,' implying it was built using human domain knowledge rather than derived statistically from training data. If it was curated using knowledge of the full corpus (including validation and test periods), it constitutes a form of future leakage—the standardization encodes vocabulary knowledge from the test period. This is subtle but real: if new product terminology emerged in months 22–24, and the curated mapping covers it, the transformer benefits from information it would not have in a true prospective deployment.

The design says preprocessing is 'fit on the training set only,' but a curated mapping is not fit on data in the same sense as vocabulary statistics. The provenance of the mapping needs to be explicitly documented.

Evidence: Document when the jargon mapping was constructed and whether it was built after examining tickets from the validation or test periods. If yes, apply it only to terms that appeared in the training window.

**Summary**

Issue 1 is the most severe because it makes the comparison between models invalid by design—the preprocessing choice systematically advantages BM25 and disadvantages the transformer. This is a silent misconfiguration that would produce plausible-looking results (BM25 might 'win' or the transformer might underperform its true capability) without any obvious error signal. Issues 2–4 are genuine but secondary; they affect the interpretation of results rather than the internal validity of the comparison.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis

### Critique 1: Stopword removal and stemming are antithetical to fine-tuning a pretrained transformer

**Critic's claim:** Applying stopword removal and stemming before BERT/DistilBERT corrupts the input distribution the model was pretrained on, degrades implicit syntactic reasoning, and creates an asymmetric disadvantage: BM25 benefits from this preprocessing while the transformer is harmed by it. The comparison therefore measures 'BM25 under favorable conditions vs. transformer under degraded conditions.'

**Assessment:** This is a valid and serious critique. I must concede it substantially.

The design rationale for 'identical preprocessing' was to ensure a like-for-like feature comparison — both models see exactly the same textual signal, so differences in performance cannot be attributed to different input representations. That is a defensible instinct in general experimental design. However, the critic is correct that this instinct is misapplied here. BERT-family models use WordPiece/BPE subword tokenization that is calibrated to natural language token distributions. Stemming prior to the tokenizer can produce out-of-vocabulary fragments or merge tokens in unintended ways; stopword removal disrupts positional and attentional contexts that transformers implicitly rely on. These are not marginal effects — they are violations of the model's input contract.

For BM25, stopword removal and stemming reduce term-mismatch noise and vocabulary sparsity, which are precisely the failure modes BM25 is most susceptible to. So the critic is correct that the preprocessing choice simultaneously helps BM25 and hurts the transformer. The claim of 'identical preprocessing as a methodological virtue' is backwards in this context.

**What should be done:** The transformer should receive minimally preprocessed text (lowercase only, plus the jargon standardization which is separately motivated). BM25 should receive its own pipeline including stemming and stopword removal. 'Fairness' in this context means each model operates under conditions optimal for its architecture, not that they receive byte-for-byte identical input. The ablation the critic proposes — comparing transformer MRR on raw vs. stemmed/stopword-removed input — would empirically confirm the magnitude of the degradation, but the theoretical basis for the concern is already solid enough to require a design change.

**What remains uncertain:** The quantitative severity is unknown without running the ablation. It is possible that for a domain-specific, jargon-heavy corpus, the degradation is smaller than for general-domain text, because much of the transformer's benefit comes from semantic embeddings rather than syntactic function-word reasoning. But this is speculative mitigation and does not justify the design as written.

**Verdict on this point:** Concede. This is a genuine flaw that invalidates the comparison as designed.

---

### Critique 2: Label definition excludes a systematic subset of hard cases

**Critic's claim:** Excluding multi-article, escalation, and unresolved tickets creates selection bias toward easy, well-defined issues. The model is trained and evaluated only on the tractable subset of the ticket population, overestimating real-world utility. This is a selection bias problem, not a labeling noise problem.

**Assessment:** Partially valid, but partially overstated. The critique is correct in identifying this as selection bias rather than noise. It is also correct that the excluded tickets represent harder retrieval cases and that deployment will encounter the full distribution. However, the design choice is not arbitrary — it is operationally grounded.

The label definition requires a single ground-truth article. Multi-article and escalation tickets lack a clean ground truth: if a ticket required three articles, which one is the 'correct' retrieval target? Forcing a label on these cases introduces a different kind of noise — ambiguous or contested ground truth — which would make evaluation less interpretable, not more. The design trades external validity (coverage of hard cases) for internal validity (label quality on included cases). This is a documented tradeoff, and the scope statement in Section 9 explicitly acknowledges it: 'This design will establish whether a fine-tuned transformer retrieves the correct resolution article with better ranking quality than BM25 on future, held-out tickets' — not on all tickets.

The critic's concern about shortcut learning (the model learning article popularity or simple keyword overlap because the training set is 'easy') is real but is partially addressed by the popularity-stratified evaluation in Section 8. If the model is exploiting popularity shortcuts, it will show inflated performance on high-frequency articles and poor performance on long-tail articles, which the stratified reporting would reveal.

**What should be done:** The design should report the exclusion rate explicitly (the critic's proposed threshold of >30% as a flag is reasonable) and break it down by ticket category. If the exclusion rate is high and concentrated, the scope limitation should be foregrounded more prominently in the expected outcome section. The current Section 9 does acknowledge scope but could be more explicit about the excluded population.

**What remains uncertain:** Whether the excluded tickets constitute a large enough fraction of production load that the performance estimates become misleading. This is an empirical question about the data.

**Verdict on this point:** Partially concede. The design choice is principled, but the scope limitation needs to be more explicitly quantified and reported. Not a fatal flaw but requires documentation.

---

### Critique 3: Near-duplicate deduplication is applied asymmetrically

**Critic's claim:** The design describes deduplication only across the train/test boundary, not within the test set itself. Repeated near-identical tickets in the test set up-weight common issue types in MRR, distorting the aggregate metric.

**Assessment:** This is a valid secondary concern. The design as written (Section 3 and Section 8, Confound 1) states that near-duplicates in training are removed from validation and test, but does not address within-split deduplication. The critic's mechanism is correct: if a common ticket type appears 50 times in the test set, it contributes 50 values to the MRR average, effectively making MRR a popularity-weighted retrieval metric rather than a uniform-ticket metric. This is a real distortion.

However, the severity depends on the corpus. Support ticket datasets typically do contain repeated common issues (password resets, login errors, etc.), so this is not a hypothetical concern. The per-category stratification in Sections 7 and 8 partially mitigates this — if one category dominates aggregate MRR, the stratified view reveals it — but it does not fully correct for within-category duplication.

**What should be done:** Apply deduplication within all splits, not just across the train/test boundary. Report the deduplication rate within splits as well. Alternatively, if preserving the natural frequency distribution is a deliberate choice (on the grounds that production load has this distribution), this should be stated explicitly and the MRR should be interpreted as a frequency-weighted metric, which is a valid but different claim.

**What remains uncertain:** Whether the natural frequency distribution of the test set is actually desirable to preserve for the business metric (agents do encounter common tickets frequently, so frequency-weighting MRR may reflect real utility). This is a design philosophy question, not purely a technical one.

**Verdict on this point:** Concede. The deduplication scope is underspecified and should be extended or the choice to preserve within-split duplicates should be explicitly justified.

---

### Critique 4: Jargon standardization mapping has ambiguous provenance

**Critic's claim:** If the curated jargon mapping was built after examining the full corpus (including validation and test periods), it constitutes subtle future leakage. The design claims preprocessing is 'fit on the training set only,' but a curated mapping is not statistically fit from data in the same sense.

**Assessment:** This is a valid and subtle concern. The distinction between 'statistically fit' and 'curated by a human who may have seen the full corpus' is real. If a domain expert built the jargon mapping after reviewing all 24–36 months of tickets, it would encode vocabulary knowledge from the test period, which a model trained only on the first 18 months would not have.

However, the severity depends on what the mapping actually contains. If the mapping covers stable, long-standing product terminology (e.g., 'HIPAA compliance' → 'HIPAA_compliance' for a healthcare SaaS product), it reflects knowledge that was available before the test period and the leakage risk is minimal. If it covers new terminology that emerged in months 22–24, the leakage concern is live.

The design's instruction that 'preprocessing transformations are fit on the training set only' implicitly applies to the jargon mapping, but the critic is correct that this claim needs to be operationalized explicitly for curated (non-statistical) artifacts. The provenance documentation requirement the critic proposes is straightforward and low-cost.

**What should be done:** Document when the jargon mapping was constructed and what sources were used to build it. If it was constructed after examining the full corpus, restrict its application to terms that appeared in the training window, or rebuild it using only training-period tickets. This is a documentation and procedural fix, not a fundamental redesign.

**What remains uncertain:** The actual provenance of the mapping and whether any test-period terminology is encoded in it. This is an empirical question about the construction process.

**Verdict on this point:** Partially concede. The concern is real and the documentation requirement is warranted, but this is likely fixable without redesigning the experiment.

---

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

Summarizing the Pass 1 analysis:

- **Issue 1 (preprocessing misconfiguration):** Full concession. This is a critical flaw. The comparison as designed is invalid because the preprocessing choice systematically advantages BM25 and disadvantages the transformer. This is not a scope limitation or a minor calibration issue — it changes what the experiment is actually measuring.

- **Issue 2 (selection bias from exclusion):** Partial concession. The design choice is principled (clean ground truth), but the scope limitation needs to be quantified and reported more prominently. Not a fatal flaw.

- **Issue 3 (within-split deduplication):** Concession. The deduplication scope is underspecified. Fixable with a procedural change or explicit justification for preserving natural frequency.

- **Issue 4 (jargon mapping provenance):** Partial concession. A real concern requiring documentation and procedural controls, not a fundamental redesign.

The critical flaw is Issue 1. It is not a performance-tuning question or a scope limitation — it is a design error that makes the core comparison (transformer vs. BM25) invalid under the stated methodology. Issues 2–4 are genuine but addressable through reporting, scoping, and procedural controls.

Given that Issue 1 is a hard concession on the experiment's central comparison, and Issues 3 and 4 are additional valid concerns (even if secondary), the overall verdict must be **critique_wins**. The methodology as written cannot produce a valid comparison between BM25 and the transformer without correcting the preprocessing pipeline. The fix is known (separate preprocessing pipelines), but the experiment needs to be redesigned before the comparison is interpretable.
all_issues_raised: Applying stopword removal and stemming to transformer inputs is an active misconfiguration that degrades BERT-family models while benefiting BM25, making the comparison invalid rather than fair.

Excluding multi-article, escalation, and unresolved tickets creates systematic selection bias toward easy cases, causing the evaluation to overestimate performance on the full production ticket distribution.
Near-duplicate deduplication is described only across train/test boundaries, not within the test set itself, allowing common ticket types to dominate MRR via repeated identical queries.

The curated jargon standardization mapping may encode vocabulary knowledge from the validation or test period if it was built after examining the full corpus, constituting a subtle form of data leakage.
all_issues_adjudicated: Applying stopword removal and stemming to transformer inputs while benefiting BM25 makes the comparison invalid by design, as the defender fully conceded this fundamentally degrades BERT's input distribution while optimizing BM25's conditions.

Near-duplicate deduplication is underspecified—applied only across train/test boundaries but not within splits—allowing common ticket types to dominate MRR via repeated identical queries, as conceded by the defender.

The curated jargon standardization mapping has ambiguous provenance and may encode vocabulary knowledge from the validation or test period, constituting subtle data leakage that requires explicit documentation and procedural controls.

Excluding multi-article, escalation, and unresolved tickets creates systematic selection bias toward easy cases, and while the design choice is principled, the scope limitation requires explicit quantification and more prominent disclosure.
